items.text: Documents and schema
The system shall store project documentation as JSON files under docs/specpad/: one project index, one SRS (requirements), and one VTP (verification tests).
Every document file shall carry a schemaVersion of "1.0" and a type of "project", "srs", or "vtp".
Every document shall include a short system name and a human-readable title.
Every SRS and VTP item shall have a required, stable id and required text; all other fields are optional.
A VTP item's result, when present, shall be one of "", "not_tested", "passed", or "failed".
Stable identity and references
Every item shall have a stable identifier that is generated once and never changes.
Item identifiers shall use a typed prefix ("r_" for requirements, "t_" for tests, "h_" for headings) followed by a random suffix.
All cross-references (a test's verifies list) shall target item ids, never human-readable code labels.
Renaming an item's human label (code) shall never alter its id or break an existing reference.
No derived or historical data
Derived values such as per-requirement test counts shall be computed on read and never stored in the files.
The files shall not store edit history (author, timestamps, change lists); git is the history layer.
Validation and governance
A shared validation module shall structurally validate each document against the v1 JSON Schema for its type.
Structural validation shall enforce required fields, value types, and the result enum, but not policy rules.
Governance shall enforce traceability: every non-heading requirement is verified by at least one test.
Governance shall enforce referential integrity: every verifies entry resolves to an existing requirement id.
Governance shall enforce expected-result presence: every non-heading test has a non-empty expected value.
Heading items shall be exempt from the traceability and expected-result governance checks.
The editor and the skill shall enforce the same governance rules from a single source of truth.
Editor
The editor shall read and write the JSON files locally via the File System Access API, with an upload/download fallback for unsupported browsers.
The editor shall display each requirement's verifying-test count, computed on read from the VTP verifies references.
The editor shall record test results and notes on VTP items.
The editor shall surface structural errors and governance warnings to the user live.
The editor shall flag a test whose verifies reference does not resolve to a requirement.
The editor shall open the document indicated by the launcher's deep-link parameters and preserve item ids across a load/edit/save round-trip.
Skill and launcher
The skill shall scaffold docs/specpad/ and create or edit documents programmatically, generating stable ids.
The skill shall validate structure and governance before reporting a task complete.
The skill shall generate a launcher (index.html) that redirects to the version-pinned hosted editor at specpad.com/v01/.
The schema reference page shall be generated at build time from the shared schema and governance modules, and the build shall fail if any schema field lacks a description.
The distributable skill shall be downloadable from the site as a zip archive rebuilt from the repository at every deploy.
Change tracking
The editor shall show which requirements and tests have changed since the latest release by diffing the working documents against the cached baseline snapshot.
Differences shall be computed by stable id, so renaming a code label or reordering items is not reported as a change.
The editor shall compute all change views; the skill shall not compute diffs, so both halves share one diff implementation and cannot disagree.
The skill shall maintain a committed, regenerable cache of raw document snapshots plus a release manifest under docs/specpad/; no computed diffs shall be stored.
The release manifest shall record, for each release, its version, git ref, date, and the author of the tagged commit.
The editor shall derive per-item attribution (the version an item was added in and last changed in) from the cached snapshots and show the responsible release author.
The editor shall present the release history from the manifest, indicating the current baseline.
Spec changes shall be associated with a development job via a current-job marker and a commit trailer, never by a field stored on individual items.
When the change-tracking cache is absent, the editor shall remain fully editable and indicate that history is unavailable.
The skill shall always cache the latest release snapshot and pull older release snapshots only on demand.
Editor — authoring
The requirements editor shall support a hierarchy via an optional per-item level (indent depth); adding a child inserts a new requirement one level deeper.
A heading row shall display a derived dotted code from its ancestor heading code segments (e.g. Shell.SaveState), not the literal word "heading".

Each requirement row shall expose a hamburger menu offering add requirement (above/below/child), add heading, indent/outdent, delete (with confirmation), and view information; outdent is disabled at level 0.
A requirement row shall offer a show-tests toggle that reveals its verifying tests inline (code, text, expected, result).
The requirements table shall not show a Hazards column; an item's hazards shall be shown read-only in the item-information modal.
The editor shall render changes since the latest release as a Word-style redline: added and modified rows highlighted, and removed rows shown inline, struck through, in place.
Requirement rows shall be reorderable by drag-and-drop.
Editor — application shell
The editor shall present an application menu bar that consolidates the app brand and primary actions, replacing the former page header and toolbar.
The menu bar's File menu shall offer New document, Open project, and Change directory, or an upload/download fallback when the File System Access API is unavailable.
When the open directory holds several projects, the menu bar brand shall switch between them via a dropdown.
Document views shall be presented as tabs (Requirements, Verification Tests, Results), extensible to further document types, with a tab disabled when its document is absent.
A footer status bar shall summarize validation — clean, or a clickable error/warning count that expands the detail messages.
Release history shall be available on demand from a menu-bar version chip that opens a dialog (or an empty state prompting a refresh).
The editor shall remember recently opened project folders via persisted directory handles, reopen them without re-picking the folder, and let the user forget a remembered folder.
Save and document state
The document tables shall be controlled: the shell owns the working document and edits are reported via an onChange callback; tables render no internal Save button.
The menu-bar Save control shall reflect unsaved changes (an indicator when dirty, disabled when clean), and Ctrl/Cmd-S shall save.
Switching project or document with unsaved changes shall prompt before discarding, and a page-close (beforeunload) guard shall warn when changes are unsaved.
Demo mode

When launched with the demo query parameter, the editor shall load the project read-only over HTTP from the hosted /demo/ directory, with the document tables, testing view, validation, version history, redlines, and the job indicator all available.
In demo mode the editor shall disable all writes (save, new document, set job) and display a visible read-only indicator.
Jobs and change attribution

The skill shall maintain an owned jobs register (<name>.jobs.json) of job records — each with a stable id, an optional code label, a title, an optional description, and an open/closed status — used when there is no external tracker.
The jobs register shall store records only; a job's change-set (the requirements, tests, and source it touched) shall be derived from git commit trailers on demand and never stored.

The active-job marker (<name>.job.json) shall name one or more active jobs as a list (with a legacy single job still read), and the skill shall write one Job: commit trailer per active job, carrying each job record's stable id.
When a jobs register exists, governance shall flag any active-job entry that does not resolve to a record in it, and any that points at a closed record.

The editor shall provide a Jobs view to list, create, and edit job records and to choose the active job(s) by multi-select, never offering a closed job for activation and allowing a closed-but-active job to be removed.
A closed job's scope shall be sealed: the skill shall not reactivate a closed job for new work but shall create a new job record instead.
Every commit that changes requirements or tests shall reference at least one open job; a git pre-push gate shall block any pushed commit that lacks a Job reference.

A source change that adds or alters a product behavior shall be accompanied by the corresponding SRS and VTP updates in the same job; a commit that changes code without a requirement shall be flagged so a requirement can be proposed (derived from the diff) and ratified or explicitly waived.

A pre-push git hook shall enforce these gates across the pushed commits — blocking any commit without a Job trailer and warning when code changed with no SRS/VTP — while skipping merge commits and history before SpecPad was adopted, and offering a per-commit waiver (a Spec: none trailer) and an emergency bypass.

In a SpecPad-governed repo, the skill shall capture requirements and verification tests as a first-class output of development — distilling the user's expressed intent into the SRS/VTP spec-first (before or alongside implementation) and attributing it to the active job — rather than documenting after the fact.

Requirement capture shall distill durable, testable statements of externally-observable behavior or governing constraints; incidental implementation detail and exploratory discussion that did not land shall not become requirements.

Captured requirements and tests shall be written autonomously and then surfaced to the user for correction, and each captured requirement shall carry a verifying test (and an automated test where the behavior is automatable).
Onboarding (specpad init)

The skill shall provide an idempotent init setup that, in one step, scaffolds docs/specpad/, generates the launcher, installs the pre-push hook (via core.hooksPath), and wires the SpecPad working-loop directive into the project CLAUDE.md — so a freshly-installed skill is fully configured with no manual steps.

init shall be safe to re-run: it shall not overwrite existing SpecPad documents, shall not duplicate the CLAUDE.md directive (guarded by a sentinel marker), and shall not clobber an existing core.hooksPath set to another directory.
init shall never overwrite an existing project CLAUDE.md; it shall append the SpecPad directive, creating CLAUDE.md only if absent.
Baseline generation (existing projects)

The skill shall provide a baseline generator that drafts an initial SRS and VTP from an existing codebase — surveying entry points, public surfaces, modules, docs, and existing tests — so SpecPad can be adopted on a project that did not start with it.

Generated requirements shall be drafts proposed for human ratification, not authoritative: each shall be tagged `draft` and surfaced for review, and deriving a requirement from code shall never bypass the user's confirmation of intent.

The generator shall distill testable behavioral requirements at the "shall" altitude — one per distinct externally-observable behavior or constraint, not implementation detail — and produce governance-clean output (every requirement carries a verifying VTP entry with an expected result).

The generator shall map requirements to existing automated tests where present (named in the VTP notes, with result reflecting the test) and shall record a behavior with no test as a not_tested gap rather than omitting it.
The generator shall report its coverage explicitly — what it covered and what it could not — rather than silently truncating.
Requirement audit (drift detection)

The skill shall provide a requirement audit that reconciles the existing SRS/VTP against the whole codebase and reports drift in both directions; the commit-time audit is the same check scoped to a staged diff.
The audit shall flag code behavior that has no requirement (missing) and propose a draft requirement, with a verifying test, derived from the code for the user to ratify.
The audit shall flag a requirement whose described behavior is no longer present or has changed in the code (stale) for update or removal, and shall never silently delete a requirement.
The audit shall flag coverage gaps — a requirement with no covering test, and a VTP notes reference to a test that no longer resolves.
The audit shall present findings as categorized proposals for the user to ratify, apply nothing destructive automatically, and report its coverage and confidence rather than silently truncating.
Jobs view (release notes)
A job record shall carry an optional type (feature or bugfix) and an optional version, used to organize the Jobs view.

The Jobs view shall present jobs as release notes — grouped by major version (unversioned jobs under "Unreleased"), then by type into Features and Bugfixes — with each job's description shown beneath its title.
Selecting a job in the Jobs list shall open a separate detail view with a way back to the list; the list and the detail are distinct views.
The Jobs tab shall be the leftmost document-view tab.
Job changes & ownership

When a job is closed, the skill shall cache its before/after SRS/VTP spec snapshots under .specpad/jobs/<id>/ (committed and regenerable) so the editor can show the job's changes; open jobs are not cached because their change-set is still growing.
A job record shall carry an owner (name and email) set from git when the job is created; this is record-level attribution, not per-item edit history.
A job's version shall be derived from the release tag whose commits contain it (Unreleased until then), not hand-set.

The Jobs view shall separate open jobs (In progress) from closed jobs (Released release notes), and a closed job's detail shall render its SRS/VTP changes from the cached before/after snapshots; an open job's changes appear only once it is closed.
Source-code traceability
Because every commit carries a Job: trailer, the skill shall derive a job's commits and code changes from git (job → commits → code) on demand, with no maintained requirement-to-code matrix.
On close, the skill shall cache a job's commit list (.specpad/jobs/<id>/commits.json, derived from the Job: trailer) so the editor can show the commits behind a job's changes.
A closed job's detail view shall list its commits (hash, subject, author, date) alongside its SRS/VTP changes.
Architecture documentation

The system shall support an optional architecture spec — an arc42 software architecture document (<name>.sad.md) plus a C4 model in Structurizr DSL (<name>.workspace.dsl) — tracked under docs/specpad/, separate from the id-keyed JSON requirements contract.
The editor shall provide an Architecture view that renders the arc42 document and presents the C4 Structurizr DSL (live C4 diagram rendering is a planned follow-up).
Architecture shall be job/release-coupled, not requirement-coupled: no requirement-to-architecture trace matrix is maintained; a job's architecture impact comes from diffing snapshots.
On close/refresh, the architecture docs shall be snapshotted into the per-job and release caches alongside the spec docs, so a job's architecture changes can be shown.

The skill shall ship two architecture profiles as templates: a generic profile (clean arc42 + C4, no safety classification) and a medical profile (IEC 62304 / FDA: per-unit classification, segregation, and architecture verification).
The init quiz shall let the user choose the project path (generic or medical) and scaffold the matching SAD template, C4 workspace, and authoring guide; generic is the default.

The generic architecture template shall provide a fuller arc42 with several C4 views and no safety classification; the medical template shall add per-unit classification, a segregation section, and an architecture-verification section.

Each profile shall ship a soft authoring guide (<name>.sad.guide.md) that the skill reads before authoring the SAD and the editor surfaces as a context panel; the guide carries tone/terminology guidance, not enforced rules.
The Architecture view shall provide Edit and Display sub-tabs (Edit hidden when read-only).
The Display tab shall render the arc42 markdown as HTML and render an embedded diagram (a draw.io SVG export) inline.
The Edit tab shall provide a syntax-highlighting editor for the arc42 markdown and the C4 source, and its edits shall be saved like other documents.
Per-job diagram change tracking shall be coarse — that the diagram file changed — not in-diagram deltas.

Diagrams shall be placed by the arc42 markdown via image references (![caption](name.svg)), so the document defines where each diagram appears — a context overview near the top, plus building block (interfaces), runtime (process), and deployment.
The Architecture Display shall render each markdown-referenced diagram SVG inline at its place in the document.
The Structurizr C4 DSL shall be optional — supported when present (loader kept; the editor shows it only when present) but not included in the default templates or scaffold.

The closed-job cache shall snapshot the architecture files (sad.md, diagram SVGs, the guide, and the optional workspace.dsl) plus a per-state manifest, so a job's architecture changes are available to the editor.

A closed job's detail shall present an Architecture changes section listing which architecture files it added, modified, or removed (coarse, file-level), plus an added/removed-line diff for the SAD markdown.
